items.question: Who still uses Multinomial or Bernoulli Naive Bayes in practice?
items.answer: ML engineers building fast text baselines, spam-filter maintainers, data scientists with small labeled corpora, and educators teaching generative classifiers all still use these models when interpretability and speed matter.

items.question: What is the core difference between Multinomial and Bernoulli Naive Bayes?
items.answer: Multinomial NB models non-negative word counts with class-conditional multinomial probabilities; Bernoulli NB models binary word presence and absence with Bernoulli probabilities for each vocabulary dimension.

items.question: When should you prefer Multinomial over Bernoulli Naive Bayes?
items.answer: Prefer Multinomial NB when term frequency carries signal—longer documents, repeated keywords, or count vectors from CountVectorizer—because each additional occurrence adds another log-likelihood term.

items.question: Where in a sklearn pipeline do these models sit?
items.answer: Typically after text vectorization: CountVectorizer (counts or binary=True) feeds MultinomialNB or BernoulliNB inside a Pipeline, with the vectorizer fit on training text only.

items.question: Why apply Laplace smoothing in text Naive Bayes?
items.answer: Smoothing prevents zero estimated probabilities for words missing from a class in training, which would otherwise zero the entire posterior when that word appears at test time.

items.question: How does Multinomial NB score a document at prediction time?
items.answer: Compute log P(y) plus the sum over vocabulary of count_i times log θ_{y,i} for each candidate class y, then choose the class with the highest log posterior (equivalently argmax posterior).

items.question: Multinomial NB vs Bernoulli NB: how do features differ?
items.answer: Multinomial NB takes integer word counts (c_i ≥ 0) and scales log-likelihood by c_i. Bernoulli NB takes x_i ∈ {0,1} and includes terms for both present and absent words, so silence on a token also carries evidence.

items.question: Multinomial NB vs logistic regression on TF–IDF: which input is natural?
items.answer: Multinomial NB pairs naturally with raw or lightly normalized count features. Logistic regression (or linear SVMs) often pairs with TF–IDF because they are discriminative and do not require a generative count model.
